description:
You are acting as a database administrator who wants every future psql session to show
    both detailed error messages and query-execution timing information, something you
    routinely enable when you are trying to optimise SQL statements.

    Objective
    ----------
    1.  Create (or overwrite, if it already exists) the file
        /home/user/.psqlrc
        so that its **entire** final contents are exactly the two lines shown below
        (no leading or trailing blank lines, spaces, or extra characters):

        \set VERBOSITY verbose
        \timing

        • Line-breaks must be Unix style “\n”.  
        • There must be **no** additional lines before, between, or after the two lines.

    2.  Accomplish step 1 by issuing a **single** shell command in the terminal.
        (Pipelining or command-chaining with “&&”, “||”, or “;” counts as a single
        compound command, which is acceptable.)

    3.  Immediately after your command executes, your terminal must print exactly
        the single word

        DONE

        on its own line (no surrounding spaces or additional output).  
        This is the only console output the automated tests will look for.

    Verification rules the grader will follow
    -----------------------------------------
    • File /home/user/.psqlrc must exist.  
    • The file must contain precisely the two required lines and nothing else.  
    • A line feed (ASCII 0x0A) must terminate each of the two lines.  
    • The stdout from your last command must be exactly the single line “DONE”.
truth:
Expected final state
    --------------------
    1.  File path: /home/user/.psqlrc  
        Contents (8 bytes + 1 byte newline + 6 bytes + 1 byte newline = 16 bytes total):

        Line 1: "\set VERBOSITY verbose\n"
        Line 2: "\timing\n"

        Hex dump:
        5c 73 65 74 20 56 45 52 42 4f 53 49 54 59 20 76
        65 72 62 6f 73 65 0a 5c 74 69 6d 69 6e 67 0a

    2.  Grader will capture stdout from the agent’s last command and compare it
        byte-for-byte to the string: "DONE\n"

    3.  The grader will tolerate any number of intermediate commands executed
        **before** the final single command, but the final command itself must
        achieve the required end-state in one go.

    4.  Permissions: default user-umask write (e.g., 0644) is fine; no executable
        bit required.
